problem_summary: 2015 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary): given a hypothetical company "ICM" with ~370 employees in a hierarchy of offices/divisions, model employee turnover (churn) and its propagation through the organization, then answer a battery of tasks — quantify turnover intention, model the contagion/spread of churn, estimate productivity loss, compute a 2-year recruiting+training budget, test whether the company can sustain an 80%-staffed state under elevated churn rates, and analyze the effect of promotion policy. This team reframed the entire problem as a "Human Capital Network" (HCN) and treated turnover as a contagion/diffusion process on that network.
models_used: Human Capital Network (HCN) reframing: two coupled graphs — a weighted Personnel Relationship Network G (370 employees as nodes, edges by same-branch/same-level/co-leadership, weights sigma vs 1) and a directed Organizational Network g (offices/divisions as nodes)

Genetic Algorithm (combinatorial optimization) to assign 370 employees to offices, maximizing a hand-built evaluation function Eval = a1*E1 + a2*E2 + a3*E3 + a4*E4 (centrality, hierarchy, equality, uniformity terms); 500 random initial solutions, gene = assignment, crossover = swapping employees between offices; implemented in MATLAB
Closeness Centrality CC_i = N / sum_j d_ij on graph G to measure how embedded/important each employee is
LeaderRank on directed graph g (with an added sink node connected to all nodes) to rank departmental importance — noted that LeaderRank of Production Manager exceeds VP despite hierarchy

Link-prediction similarity via a revised Resource Allocation (RA) Index on the weighted graph, s_xy = sum over common neighbors z of (w_xz + w_yz)/k(z), used both as turnover-contagion 'closeness' (structural equivalence) and to match employees to vacancies
Logistic-curve time-decay of churn impact: contagion factor C1i = sum over churned employees of [e^(-beta(t-tj)) / (1 + e^(-beta(t-tj)))] * s_ij — past churns' influence attenuates over time

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to weight the four turnover-intention factors (C1 contagion, C2 centrality, C3 salary, C4 promotion prospect) into T = a1*C1 + a2*C2 + a3*C3 + a4*C4; pairwise comparison matrix solved in MATLAB giving weights (0.3084, 0.1482, 0.1445, 0.3989) with consistency check CI=0.0045, CR=0.05<0.1
Discrete-time stochastic simulation of HCN dynamics over a 24-month horizon, coupling three sub-processes: turnover, promotion, recruitment
Poisson process for churn counts and for recruitment arrivals: P(n=k)=e^-lambda lambda^k/k!, with recruitment rate lambda = 1/MT_j (inverse of median time-to-recruit for level j)
Weighted random selection of who churns, using each node's AHP turnover-intention T as the sampling weight
Truncated Gaussian distribution of employee service time per level to seed promotion eligibility, with service-time thresholds per position (SM=8, JM=5, ES=3, IS=2, EE=1, IE=0 years)
Productivity model Productivity = sum_i lambda_i * P_i * e_i (lambda_i = office LeaderRank, P_i = salary/ability proxy, e_i = efficiency inversely related to turnover intention)

Cost models: recruiting cost RC_all = sum of median recruit cost over new workers, training cost TC_all = sum of median training cost over all nodes, expressed in units of sigma (sigma left symbolic since dollar figures weren't given)
Multiplex / multilayer network extension (Task 6): overlaying the HCN with a same-hometown network, with Jaccard coefficient J = |a∩b|/|a∪b| to quantify inter-layer link overlap

Euclidean-distance position-matching for HR (Task 8): best position k = argmin_j ||Phi_k - e|| between an employee's evaluation vector e and each position's requirement vector Phi_k; plus average clustering coefficient as an organizational-unity metric
key_techniques: Problem reframing as the core move: converting a vague HR/management prompt into a network-science problem, which unlocked an entire toolbox (centrality, link prediction, diffusion, multilayer nets)

Bridging a data gap with optimization: the problem gave no office assignments, so they SYNTHESIZED the missing network structure by formulating assignment as combinatorial optimization and solving with a GA, then used the GA output as input data for everything downstream

Treating turnover as a contagion/diffusion process on the network rather than as independent per-employee events — capturing the 'indirect effect' (a churn raises neighbors' intention) that the problem explicitly asked about
Layering AHP on top of network metrics: network analysis produces raw factor scores (C1..C4), AHP supplies defensible weights with a consistency check — a clean separation of 'measure' vs 'weigh'
Logistic-curve temporal attenuation so contagion impact decays with time, giving the dynamics realistic transient spikes that settle back

Monte-Carlo-style discrete-time simulation as the unifying engine: one simulation framework answers Tasks 2-5 (productivity loss 9.8%, budget 30.01 sigma, 80%-sustainability failure at 25%/35% churn, promotion effects)
Using salary as a proxy for ability (P_i) — a simple, defensible surrogate when ability isn't directly measurable
Sensitivity analysis on the most assumption-laden parameters (the truncated-Gaussian service-time mean mu and variance sigma) to show conclusions are robust to the weakest inputs
Keeping units symbolic (sigma) when the problem withheld concrete cost numbers, so the budget answer stays well-defined and clean rather than fabricated
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper won O-award because it made one bold, well-executed conceptual leap — recasting a fuzzy HR turnover question as a dynamic network-science problem — and then carried that frame consistently through every single task. That coherence is rare and exactly what judges reward: a single Human Capital Network model + one discrete-time stochastic simulation engine answers Tasks 2 through 5, so the paper reads as one integrated story rather than five disconnected mini-solutions. It also directly nailed the part of the prompt most teams botch: the INDIRECT effect of turnover (contagion raising coworkers' intention), modeled concretely via RA-index similarity and a logistic time-decay, not hand-waved. The methodology is sophisticated but each tool is justified and cited (centrality, LeaderRank, RA index, AHP, multiplex networks, all with references), and the AHP consistency check (CR=0.05) plus the sensitivity analysis on the weakest assumptions signal scientific maturity. Finally it produced crisp, decision-useful numbers — 9.8% productivity drop, 30.01 sigma budget, demonstrable failure to hold 80% staffing at 25-35% churn — which is what a manager (and a judge) wants to see. The team also handled the missing-data trap creatively by generating the assignment structure via GA instead of giving up.
section_highlights: Structure mirrors the modeling pipeline and is easy for a judge to follow: Assumptions/Notations → HCN model (assignment via GA → build G and g → turnover-intention factors → AHP weighting → productivity/cost) → Dynamics (turnover/promotion/recruitment) → Simulation Results mapped task-by-task → Sensitivity → Multilayer extension → HR discussion → Strengths/Weaknesses. The Summary sheet is strong: it states a concrete headline result per task (e.g., "budget requirement of 19.14 sigma for recruiting and 10.87 sigma for training... adding up to 30.01 sigma", "ICM can't sustain its 80% full status"). Each result section is explicitly tagged to the task it answers ("For Task 2(1)...", "For Task 3..."), making it trivial for a judge to verify completeness against the prompt. Figures carry real interpretive weight — turnover-intention propagation snapshots two months apart, worker-count decay curves at 18%/25%/35% churn, productivity and cost trajectories — and the narrative reads results off them rather than just captioning. Notably the consistency check and sensitivity tables are given their own visible space, signaling rigor. (Caveat: this is a recovered/OCR'd markdown version with garbled passages, placeholder figure captions like "Figure 6. Task 2.1", a broken notation row, and truncated sentences — the structural skeleton and quantitative claims are the reliable signal, not the prose fidelity.)
pitfalls_or_limitations: Fabricated/assumed network structure: because the problem gave no actual personnel relationships, the entire HCN is built on GA-assigned offices plus three hand-chosen connection rules — the model's realism is only as good as those assumptions, and a different assignment could shift results

Many parameters set by 'common sense' with no data: Eval weights (0.5,0.5,-0.05,-0.05) tuned by 'some experiments with random assignments'; salary/promotion factors hard-set to {1,0,0} and {0,1,0}; contagion decay beta unspecified — the team admits this in Weaknesses

Coarse aggregation: middle managers and low-level employees are modeled as distinct individuals but reported only as lumped groups, so the paper can't see within-group heterogeneity (acknowledged weakness)

Sensitivity analysis is thin and internally suspicious — Table 3 (sigma) and Table 4 (mu) contain IDENTICAL numbers, which looks like a copy-paste error and undermines the robustness claim; only two parameters were varied, not the high-leverage AHP weights or contagion decay
Salary-as-ability proxy is convenient but questionable (tenure, role, and market effects confound it); same-hierarchy-same-salary assumption flattens real variation

The multiplex-network and Euclidean position-matching sections (Tasks 6-8) are sketched conceptually but not actually simulated or quantified — they read as 'future work' bolted on rather than executed

Some derivations are stated, not shown (e.g., LeaderRank computation is announced then skipped: 'we calculate the LeaderRank Value LR as follows:' with no formula), and the 80%-sustainability and 9.8% productivity numbers depend on simulation settings that aren't fully specified for reproducibility
Heavy reliance on a single 24-month simulation horizon and unstated random-seed/replication count — no confidence intervals on the headline numbers
reusable_patterns: The 'reframe the soft problem as a network' play: when a prompt is about people/organizations/influence/spread, model entities as nodes and relationships as weighted edges, then reach for centrality, link-prediction, and diffusion — instantly turns a mushy question into a quantifiable one

Generate missing data with optimization: if the problem withholds a needed structure (assignments, layout, schedule), formulate it as an objective + constraints and solve with a metaheuristic (GA/Tabu/SA), then feed the output forward as if it were given data

Two-stage 'measure then weight' design: use domain/network metrics to compute raw factor scores, then use AHP (with the CI/CR consistency check) to combine them into a single index — defensible, and the consistency check is cheap rigor that impresses judges

One simulation engine, many tasks: build a single discrete-time stochastic simulator (Poisson arrivals/departures, weighted random events, per-period state update) and answer multiple sub-questions by reading different outputs from the same runs — maximizes coherence and saves time
Logistic / exponential time-decay kernel for any 'influence that fades' (contagion, advertising, rumor, alumni ties): sum_j kernel(t - t_j) * similarity_ij is a reusable template

RA-index / structural-equivalence similarity as a general 'how alike/influential are these two nodes' measure on weighted graphs — reuse for contagion strength, recommendation, or vacancy-to-person matching
Symbolic units when numbers are withheld: answer in units of an undefined constant (sigma) so the result stays exact and honest instead of inventing figures

Sensitivity-analysis-as-defense: pre-empt the 'your parameters are made up' critique by explicitly varying the most assumption-laden inputs and showing the conclusion holds — but DO it carefully (don't duplicate tables) and prioritize the high-leverage parameters (here, the AHP weights and decay rate would have been the ones to test)
Task-tagged results sections ('For Task N:') that map one-to-one onto the prompt's requirements, making completeness auditable at a glance
